RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 4: Events and Event Listeners
Day 4 of my reincarnation. Yesterday I got selectors figured out, so the next thing on the list is events and event listeners.

There are a ton of different events out there, so I'll just list the common ones. An event is basically your action — clicking a button, dragging the mouse, even dragging a window around. All of those are events, sometimes called event handlers. The whole point is to listen for any action the user takes. But the listening part is a separate story (more on that in a sec).
The common events you'll run into are things like
— these are all JavaScript events. Back in the early days of web dev you might've seen events like
were mostly written directly in the HTML, like this:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Hardly anyone writes it this way anymore. The main problem is it's a maintenance nightmare — just changing one event can have you hunting around forever. So these days most devs put their events in a JavaScript file instead. That said, you'll still bump into the inline style now and then.

In short, an event listener is me "hiring someone to watch for a specific thing you do" — like watching for the moment you click a button, or tracking the path your mouse moves. I mentioned that these days we write event listeners in a JavaScript file, so how do you actually write one? The syntax is
, and it registers a specific event onto the element you pass in. That's still kind of vague, so let's look at an example.

Say we want clicking a button to fire an event and have it say hi to me. The JavaScript would look like the example below. But first we have to use a selector to grab the DOM element we want to listen on — otherwise how would it know which one you mean?
// Grab the element we want to listen on
// First argument is the event to listen for, second is what runs when it fires, third one I'll cover later
// Note: JavaScript events don't need the 'on' prefix
That third argument is the so-called
— and you can either fill it in or leave it out. Leaving it out defaults to

So right now the display looks like this ↓
should only fire the box you actually clicked. But what happens if we set the third argument to
You can see that even though we clicked
popped up too. Now what if we switch the third argument back to the default
This time the output is reversed, and it still propagates through. So
for that third argument mean these two things:
- fires from the target element outward
- fires from the outer layer inward to the target element
If I draw it out, here's the default
Of course, the third argument has more formal names:

For event bubbling, let me put it in isekai terms: picture yourself on a big grassland watching a rabbit (the target element), then looking out into the rest of the grassland (the outer layers) for other rabbits.
For event capturing, it's the other way around — you start from the big grassland (outer layer) and search inward for a single rabbit (the target element).
Sometimes, though, we don't want bubbling or capturing to happen. JavaScript gives us a piece of syntax to deal with this:
. It stops the current event from propagating any further through the capturing and bubbling phases. Once you add it, no matter how you click

Day 4 of my reincarnation. After getting selectors down yesterday, the next thing to learn is events and event listeners. We'll cover what an event is, how to wire up addEventListener, and the third argument nobody talks about — event bubbling vs. event capturing — plus how to stop it with stopPropagation.
